comments.content: What are your thoughts on the Akashic Records?  What are they, how are they accessed, and what can we learn from them?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The Akashic Records were a notion circulated by Theosophical writers and practitioners in the late 19th and early 20th centuries: the idea that everything that has ever happened down here in the world of matter has been imperishably engraved in the akasha, the fifth element of Hindu cosmology, more or less corresponding to the element of Spirit, and the follow-on idea that if you developed the skills of seership you could tap into the Akashic Records and see the truth about what happened in any past era you care to name. That was the theory. In practice, the people who cultivated astral seership (which is what they were talking about, though they didn't know it) got a mix of actual glimpses of the past, their own preconceptions, and the popular notions of their era all jumbled up together, and ended up writing a great deal of very colorful nonsense as a result -- I'm thinking here among many others of the Theosophical writer who talked earnestly about 12-foot-tall hermaphroditic Lemurians walking tame plesiosaurs on leashes. If there are levels of being where the objective truth about the past is infallibly engraved, they seem to be above the normal reach of the human soul at this stage of our evolution.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It is indeed. What's more, as I'll be covering in a post on the main blog sometime soon, the Theosophists basically invented fantasy fiction, and most of the fantasy novels of the golden age -- including Tolkien's The Lord of the Rings -- are dripping Theosophy out of every pore. It was when the occult influence went away that fantasy got dull.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It didn't happen by accident. Occultism was driven out of fantasy and science fiction in the late 1970s and early 1980s by the skeptic movement. The first science fiction convention I ever attended, in 1977, still had workshops on reading tarot cards and the like -- those stopped being allowed shortly thereafter. Many publishers stopped buying SF novels in which psychic phenomena featured too extensively -- before then, it was all over the place; pick up an old Andre Norton novel sometime and see how much psychism you find -- and fantasy magic got Harry Potterized, with all references to actual occultism scrubbed out.

comments.content:
Dear J.M. Greer

1a) In your reply to my question last week about possible kamea of Uranus and Neptune, you said that they would have to be assigned squares of orders 2 and 1, which of course is a dead end for kamea. But can we then definitely say that Uranus would be assigned the number 2, and Neptune assigned 1? That seems to be progress of a sort.
1b) As another line of investigation: For the seven classical planets we have traditional metal assignments - lead, tin, iron, gold, copper, mercury, silver. Could Uranus and Neptune have corresponding metals, and if so, have any of these been established yet?

2) Of the Medieval, Renaissance, and early modern grimoires, what are some of the most worthwhile for the mage in training or occultist in training to study and meditate about?

3) Are the Olympian spirits (as in the Arbatel) a different class of being from the planetary Intelligences, Spirits, angels and so on? What in their nature distinguishes them from those other classes of beings?

Thank you for hosting this forum and for your replies,
Regards,
Josh Rout

replies.title: Re: Uranus & Neptune, Grimoires, Olympian Spirits, Pacts With Satan

3) They appear to be a different class of being. That's about as much as can be said for certain. You can always summon them and ask!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Going to rec Llewellyn's Complete Book of Lucid Dreaming. I got a few lucid dreams after a month or so of work with their process, but went on to other things.

The basic process was approximately:
1. Keep a dream journal to help you actually remember the dreams
2. Set your alarm 2-3 hours before you plan on actually getting up, then focus on your intention to realize you're dreaming and go back to bed.

The one tip that really helped me was to start doing "dream checks" during the day - discretely trying to put a hand through the mirror or checking whether if you look at a sign, look away, and look back that the text remains the same. This gets your mind constantly checking to see if its in a dream and that habit continues while you're sleeping. Also adding an affirmation helped as well, which our host has covered elsewhere on here.

comments.title: Was Robespierre possessed by a demon/demons?
comments.content: Dear JMG, do you think Maximilien Robespierre was possessed by demons?  I think it seems very odd that he went from "incorruptible" and upholding the Declaration of the Rights of Man to catching guillotine fever and the forcing Cult of the Supreme Being upon the French people.

replies.title: Re: Was Robespierre possessed by a demon/demons?
replies.content: No, not at all. The cult of reason almost always ends in a heap of corpses, because people who are intoxicated with their own supposed rationality are unable to assess their own beliefs critically. When the world fails to behave according to the "self-evident truth" of their preconceptions, all they can do is turn to force. That's why Marxist regimes so reliably end up killing people on an industrial scale.  Marxist economics inevitably fail -- as you'd expect from a scheme cooked up by an intellectual -- but Marxists can't assess their own beliefs critically, so they turn to violence, and more violence, and still more violence, until you've got Pol Pot's Cambodia, Stalin's purges and prison camps, or the tens of millions of corpses left by Maoism in China.

replies.title: Re: Was Robespierre possessed by a demon/demons?
replies.content: What skills/habits should one cultivate so that one is able to asses their own beliefs critically?

replies.title: Re: Was Robespierre possessed by a demon/demons?
replies.content: Reflection, discursive meditation, and the ability to laugh at your own folly.

replies.content: Yes. I've fixed it.

replies.title: Re: Was Robespierre possessed by a demon/demons?
replies.content:
Robespierre was a Deist, like large numbers of educated people in the 18th century, and his views were not very different from those of the founders of your country. The difference is that in France at the time the Catholic Church was bitterly reactionary, and actively conspiring with the King and the aristocracy to foment counter-revolution and bring foreign armies into France to put down the revolution and restore absolute monarchy. The response of Robespierre and others was to try to promote a depoliticised religion, at a time when the established one was deeply compromised and often bitterly resented. (Many of the popular risings in the countryside were directed initially against the Church and the aristocracy).
Robespierre remains a very controversial figure even now, but it’s fair to say that modern historians have dismissed many of the wilder accusations against him, put about by liberal historians then and since as a way of discrediting the earlier, popular phase of the revolution, which effectively ended with the murder of Robespierre and many of his radical colleagues in 1794. After that, the middle class liberals took power, and the original idea - that people like you and me have importance - was swiftly abandoned.
Interestingly, quite a lot of the hostile reaction to the revolution was based on the idea that, since deposing and executing an anointed ruler was so obviously contrary to God, the revolution must obviously have been not just a plot by Freemasons, which was widely believed, but by devil-worshippers too.

comments.title: Titles telling tales
comments.content:
John Michael, what deep spell has your writing cast over me? Once, I could glide blithely through life reveling in the trite pablum on offer all about me. But now, I can hardly scan the headlines of even the alternative press or the titles on the library's shelves without seeing a whole history of ideas embedded in the very words employed to compel me. As for watching modern movies, it has become almost painful to be subjected to the exceptionally limited plot lines repeated ad nauseum, driving home preferred propaganda points. What did you do with that naive fool I so well remember, who enjoyed watching rom-coms and fantasizing about his life progressing... always progressing...

Today I stopped into a local thrift shop and browsed through the books. I could barely get past the title Making Globalism Work! It was one of those business improvement books promising to put the reader at the front of the pack in terms of looting wealth out of the global economy. But I couldn't help reading it as a desperate plea for everyone to pretend that an unworkable disaster might somehow not be so bad -- like Making Fascism Work, or Making Child Abuse Work. Then there was the 1960's title "Today's Isms". Good Lord, how long have they been trying to peddle their drivel? What similar guides to right-think and Newspeak were being published when the enclosure laws were getting implemented? Don't these "experts" ever get tired of playing the endless apologist for their failed theories?

What are your unsuspecting readers to do when we wake up one day surprised to find ourselves in minds that can think for themselves? Awareness has become a decidedly mixed blessing just now when craziness and disconnection are cynically being turned up to their highest volumes. No wonder so many Hippies wanted to just "drop out" during the stressful protest years!

-Christophe

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)

1) This is why it helps to have some philosophical training before you start making grand statements about spiritual systems. The human mind can't make direct contact with the world. What you think of as the world "right there in front of you" is being constructed by your mind, moment by moment according to unconscious patterns that are partly hardwired into your nervous system by evolution, partially structured by your culture, and partly a result of your own life experience. When people decide to turn away from conscious thought and embrace life "as it is," they're simply embracing the productions of their own unconscious mental processes in an uncritical manner. The reason paths like Cabalism and Neoplatonism work with abstract concepts is not to flee from material existence -- it's a way of understanding your own role in creating what you call "material existence" so you can engage with it, and with all the other planes of being, in a way that's more richly conscious and less constrained by your own presuppositions.

2) Why do you want to encourage yourself in feeling proud? Why do you think it's a good idea to live your desires? These aren't rhetorical questions.

comments.title: On circles
comments.content: I have been wondering about the directional correspondences of magic circles and directional wheels. Western occultism primarily use the Golden Dawn correspondences but other systems use different directional layouts. For instance, in Chinese metaphysics water is in the north, wood(spirit) in the east, fire in the south, metal(air) in the west, and earth at the center. Native American medicine wheels also have different methods and the ancient Greeks also appear to have used a unique setup of air in the north, fire in the east, earth in the south, and air in the west. My question is about the significance of elemental directions. Are the energetic patterns of directions objective influences? Can you program any set of correspondences into your subconscious and have it work for you? Perhaps different correspondences are better or worse in different situations such as pre-heaven and post-heaven bagua are used differently?

replies.title: Re: On circles
replies.content: In my experience, you can choose any set and work with it, and get the same results. That being the case, if you ever expect to work magic with anyone else, it helps to have the same system of directional correspondences -- thus the convenience of the Golden Dawn set in the western world.

replies.title: Re: On circles
replies.content:
Shouldn't we consider also that the term 'elements,' in this case, was an old mistranslation of the Chinese Xing (行), which would more accurately be seen as phases or processes of energetic transformation? The Elements approach seems to focus on essence or Being, and the Xing or Phase approach focuses on process or Becoming. Note that Chinese systems don't use Elementals ('Phasementals'?) - they don't care about the essential nature of the spirits they interact with, only about their doings and functions. The same thing applies to the energetic aspects of human viscera or organs.
So in terms of the broad directional energies, the oriental model would capture a different aspect from the occidental approach - it would be a 'different part of speech,' magically.
Does any of that seem right?
~Josh Rout

replies.title: Re: On circles
replies.content: There are certainly Taoist writers who agree with you.

comments.title: Esoteric Aikido no news and other things
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Two questions/comments this week:

1. About aikido and its esoteric aspects - this article was very interesting:

https://kogenbudo.org/great-aikido-aikido-greats/

It's by Ellis Amdur who is a well known Western historian of Japanese martial arts, and speaks fluent Japanese and does not just aikido but has practiced some very obscure Japanese martial arts for decades.

Most of the article is him talking about the great practitioners of aikido and why they were good, and while that is interesting to an aikidoka, I am posting it because he starts talking about some of the esoteric practices of Japanese martial artists towards the end of the article (from Section 7: "Stepping off the Edge of the World" where he talks about how O-Sensei and other martial artists "hacked their nervous system" as part of their legendary and almost unbelievable abilities: in Ueshiba's case, involving mystic rituals from the Shingon Mikkyo and something called "Chinkon kishin" which he says is a form of shamanism and involves allowing a deity to possess you.

Then he says this about the practices of Japanese warriors till the 20th century, which have now been lost:

"There was a ubiquitous practice amongst warriors who believe combat was their only means of immortality through creation of a name beyond death. Bare remnants of this remain today. This is done by means of a melding of shamanistic practices like vision quests, spirit possession, etc. combined with martial training. (13) It’s dangerous—both physically and psychologically. Often it included starvation, sleeplessness, exposure to the elements, physical training beyond exhaustion, and physical mortification. The end goal of this is an analogue to the berserker state (and this is not a chaotic excited delirium—rather, it is going in the zone, like Michael Jordan in the last seconds of the game, but in this case, in the service of killing). Ueshiba did this—and it required many hours for many years. I’m not recommending this for anyone."

Later on, Amdur says the following in a foot note which brings me to my actual question:

"When I first posted this section on Facebook, I got a question whether I was referring to ‘hacking’ into one’s own nervous system or that of another person. In this essay, I am talking about one’s own. Hacking someone else is the meaning of kiaijutsu (which requires, by the way, hacking your own to be any good at it).Some koryu are truly sophisticated at this. Araki-ryu, which is my main study for the past 45 years is one. I distilled a modernized version of this as part of a project for DARPA, along with cognitive scientist, Robert Hubal. The results are in two books, The Coordinator and The Accord agent (two versions of the same material for different audiences)."

Question: In addition to being a general comment about the fascinating esoteric roots of aikido (I've posted before a few months about aikido's esoteric aspects here), my question is: what Amdur is talking about here basically sounds like magic to me? He's talking about "hacking your own nervous system" and then he talks about "influencing others nervous system" and has written manuals on conflict resolution. Isn't this basically another way of stating the famous Dion Fortune definition of magic? Does that mean that (for example) hostage negotiators and people who resolve conflicts etc are using magic?

2. On a completely different note, I was going to post today anyway and mention that I stopped reading the news last weekend, over a week ago(we don't have a tv so I don't watch the news anyway but used to read a fair bit every day on various news websites), and also discuss news on various fora.

My wife has been saying for a few weeks now that I have started to get really angry and "rage" about the coronavirus lockdown and how we are destroying our society for a virus that isn't that serious. I never raised my voice (and my wife agrees with me anyway) but I certainly allowed it to get to me.

I'm not sure exactly what changed last weekend, but about two days before we were due to get news of more restrictions, something just clicked in my mind and I decided to go off news cold turkey. I think it was last Saturday or Sunday - not sure.

I've never felt better and calmer than in the last ten days.

Some news still comes in by osmosis - people mention it or I read in passing on sites like Ecosophia or glance at headlines on newsstands. And I've had to check specific things (like details of the new restrictions) once or twice. I also (just today) started back with one weekly newsmagazine. But I've almost completely stepped out of the constant daily news cycle and it feels so much better.

My opinions haven't changed - if I let myself dwell on the lockdowns and the damage caused by them, I can still make myself angry and upset (every time I walk the streets and see the number of closed shops and restaurants and talk to small business owners etc).

I don't really miss the news to be honest. I just feel calmer and more relaxed and it's easier to to think about something else if I find myself getting angry. And I'm still perfectly well informed. And more time to read more important things. I don't want to become a hermit so I am still reading one or two weekly magazines, but no desire to read constant online news - even a daily newspaper is preferable (although I don't need it).

I am trying to remind myself that even though hard times are here, and will last for a while, "this too shall pass" and there will be a recovery and in the interim, my family and my career are (luckily, due to the nature of my work), not affected, and even if the lockdown restrictions keep getting worse, I can focus on things that don't involve social contact, like reading more, my own solo aikido drills and practice plus physical fitness, spending more time with my kids and so on, and then I can go back to more social activities when recovery happens. I can still move towards my goals in other words (and we're not at full lockdown here yet, but it's getting worse).

This is partly a distraction issue - as in, I distract myself during lockdown with other activities - but also occurs to me that it is a Law of Limitation issue - that I am using the limits imposed by coronavirus restrictions to move in other directions (greater physical fitness+aikido skill etc). Also, I remember reading your points about how the environmental movement failed and change comes from within and must be led by myself first. So in addition to the above, if *I* can stop succumbing to Covid hysteria (on either side -whether that is worry about the virus, OR the rage and frustration at the overreaction to it), and focus on living the life I want to live, as normally as possible in the circumstances, then I am an example of that change to everyone around me - "Be The Change You Want To See" etc. In fact, I think the contents of the paragraph above are something I should meditate on.

The only thing I really miss is one particular discussion forum - I may go back to that and simply avoid any topics relating to current affairs.

Oh - and I saw on the other blog some talk about a potential collective change of consciousness last weekend. I can't say I felt anything about an external tension resolving or anything, but I do think it is very interesting that my out-of-the-blue decision to stop watching or reading news happened on the same weekend (and maybe the same day). I wonder if it is connected.

replies.title: Re: Esoteric Aikido no news and other things
replies.content:
1) Amdur uses the language of "hacking your nervous system" either because he's found that such terms communicate well to Western students, or because that's how he himself interprets it. You can bet your bottom dollar that those aren't the terms that Uyeshiba and the other practitioners of the system used! That is to say, yes, it's magic.

2) Excellent! The media exist to keep you in a state of endless upset, and the only way out that they present to you is buying products to feel better. Pulling the plug is a better option.

comments.title: Historic Objects
comments.content: I've been drawn to historic objects and artifacts since I was a small child. (My daycare lady told my parents, when I was three, that she was concerned about me because while the other kids were playing in her back yard I was looking for Civil War artifacts) I don't really know where the interest came from. At that age I would tell people I wanted to be a Confederate soldier when I grew up and would dress like one for Halloween. That aspect I think I've figured out as I think I know who I was in a past life-I was an officer in the NC cavalry and got killed on the third day of Gettysburg. The aspect of historic objects "finding" me I haven't been able to figure out. The last Japanese flag to fly over the Philippines "found" me when I was 13 due to a brief encounter with the man who captured it giving it to me. I found a collection of photos of a WW1 fighter pilot at a flea market when I was in middle school. Several years later a friend gave me a bundle of condolence letters, he found at a garage sale, written to the parents of a young man who was killed when his B-17 was shot down over Italy in WW2. On closer inspection I realized the photos of the WW1 pilot were the father of the young man from WW2. I have quite a few other strange occurances like that where objects come back together. Today I am the guardian for a huge collection of historic artifacts, some military some not. I feel like I am supposed to fullfill some kind of role for them but I'm not sure what? When I got out of the Army in 2015 I received a degree in Public History but was unable to find work in the field. (This may be for the best as the museum/public history realm has sadly become thoroughly woke and the domain of AWFLs) I don't think donating stuff to a museum right now is a good plan because as budget cuts hit I imagine a lot of museums will begin selling off their collections to maintain the salaries of the curators. This will probably be done under the guise of decolonizing the museum or some other woke jargon. So I think I'm supposed to be the guardian of the objects in my care. Is there an occult path I should be following? It has taken me quite a few years to understand that there may be some metaphysical forces at play with all of this. When I first found your blog about 11 years ago I was like "this guy is really smart but he believes in magic and multiple gods, snicker snicker." comments.title: Pair affirmation
comments.content:
Hi,

I feel a bit awkward about bringing up again a subject that has been exhaustively discussed several times, but here goes.

Last year after your affirmation post, I asked if it was a good idea to use an affirmation for a pair to return to a fulfilling sex life. You recommended not to do that without full consent from the other partner, and I saw the point in that and stopped using it.

One year later, the situation has not really changed, but some months ago my wife started talking about ways to recover her libido, and specifically began to take some plant-based preparations. While these did not seem to have any effect, I took this as as a sign to talk with her about asking for a fuller sex life between us. She said yes, but that I shouldn't ask for anything too frequent (more than once a week...). After trying out a lot of different formulae, I settled on a very simple one, that would in English be approximately "We love having sex (with each other)".


I used the affirmation as often as possible, maybe approaching a hundred times a day, for a week or two, but gradually stopped because of misgivings. You said (talking generally) that affirmations should be accompanied by strong visualization. In this case, the visualization feels a bit too much like an unhealthy obsession for me to be entirely comfortable with it. On the other hand, I wonder if I should simply have kept up with the practice. On her side, she stopped taking the medication after a while and hasn't come back to the topic on her own (in fact blocks discussion). When we do try to have sex, it seems very disjointed and doesn't really work for both of us.

For context, we rather suddenly stopped having good sex, or sex more than a few times a year, six years ago after the birth of our daughter, when she was 34 and I was 38. Right now, she is extremely stressed out with professional deadlines. It always seems as if waiting a bit, things might get better on their own, but they never do. Though I try to be patient during the day, I woke up in the middle of last night in emotional turmoil. Strangely enough, analyzing the emotions calmed me down a bit this time; I was able to detect anger, desperation, regret, sadness, resentment and several grades of shame. On some former occasions, the affirmation had been helpful to calm me down.

So in short: do you think this is a case of simply pushing ahead with the affirmation, trusting that my wife is in principle onboard with the idea, though it does not seem to be high priority for her, or do you suggest any other path?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Pair affirmation
replies.content: This is the kind of question you need to take to a counselor or therapist, not to a guy with Aspergers syndrome for whom the intricacies of human relationships aren't exactly easy!

comments.title: Musings on asana yoga
comments.content:
Good morning JMG and everyone, Last week on MM there was a comment about the current state of yoga being debased. Yes, it is. And not only debased, but tattooed, coiffed in dreads and stuffed into overpriced plastic pants!

As a very longtime student and teacher, lately I’ve been doing a lot of thinking, influenced by our CosDoc discussions and the many relevant comments made by this commentariat.

In our recent musings on the Law of Limitations, where Dion Fortune tells us that beginners need to feel the great movements of energy around us that are available to everyone. This is appropriate for the stone cold beginners in yoga. How can we work with prana until we know it is there and experience it? My beginners spend a lot of time getting in touch with their physical and etheric bodies. But these days yoga stops there, similarly as does the current Neo-pagan movement as pointed out by JMG. Now, it’s all about energy flowing here, there and everywhere. Perhaps exemplified by the insistence of calling every class “flow” this and “flow” that. Another example of debasement is the insistence of refusing to call proud postures by their beautiful Sanskrit names, like calling Uttitha Hasta Pandangusthasana.....extended kangaroo....are we in kinder? Really?

As we move on to really practice yoga, we must avail ourselves of the Law of Limitations or it is only strip mall aerobics. Each class is a limitation on what we can do during the time allowed. Each asana (posture) itself is an extreme limitation of movement. We each have our unique limitation within each posture which we must accept to an extent. With muscle flexibility, we may slowly extend our limits with patience and focus. With skeletal limits we must accept the limits of the current physical body with which we are gifted. For example, if the shape of your hip bones is not exactly right, you will never get into lotus no matter how hard you work. This requires usually a limitation of the ego ;)

Only when we begin to limit our bodies and our breaths (with correct yogic breathing) can we focus like a laser to set our minds free to pursue our heart’s desires.

Another thought....recently someone here mentioned the occurrence of a student spontaneously beginning to cry during practice. Back in the day, in Iyengar class (my long time teacher is now in her 90’s so it was really old school!) this was just something that happened occasionally and it was a good thing, so cry it out and we will hug and cry and laugh together after practice......Perhaps as the student has become absorbed into an extremely limited state, something, some little sorrow or heartbreak no longer has any space and it’s time to let it go. As Dion Fortune says we must ruthlessly eliminate the things peripheral to our goals. The physical and etheric body is spontaneously casting out that which is longer necessary.

To sum it up...to restore asana yoga to the beautiful discipline it is, perhaps we need a lot less solar energy (big egos, big competition) going on here and a lot more Saturnian energy! while still having a positive, jolly good time at practice.

Thanks for indulging my thoughts kind teacher! Apologies for the length...once I get on my soapbox.... ;)

SundaraYogaShala

replies.title: Re: Musings on asana yoga
replies.content: Sorry, I don’t have any recommendations for on-line classes.....traditional teachers tend to be video and internet shy :) and feel very strongly about in person practice.  However you would be very well served, especially as a beginner to get a copy of Asana Pranayama Mudra Bandha by Swami Satyananda Saraswati. His instruction is very sound, clear and well explained.  My elderly Iyengar teacher always taught us, if in yoga practice, you only learn how to breathe properly and stand up straight, everything else is icing on the cake. Take it slow you you will be fine!

comments.content: I was told by an industry insider that the quality of Christian music is controlled by the money people who want the same exact kind of sound all the time, as if they are factory owners who have the right to demand that artists make a consistent quality, manufactured product that is ‘guaranteed to sell’ to a captive audience. So if the 2nd Religiosity breaks their stranglehold on the creative work being done, it will indeed be something of a miracle; because it will have to negate the overriding power of the corporate conglomerate muzak mindset: dull, conventionalized taste, deep pockets and all. Or else people will have to make music outside the clanking confines of money machine and  work only for the glory of God. Is there any point in asking for concerted prayer to free the concert from the grip of money god?  Or is it a matter of individual struggle to be heard above and beyond worldly folly for false gain?  How does money get so much power over modern minds? Patrons of the arts in other eras sponsored many masterpieces. replies.title: Re: Christian Pop Music
replies.content: Ah, so they're held prisoner by the same corporate chains that shackle pop music generally these days. Prayer, individual or collective, is certainly appropriate; a god who could free the Israelites from Egypt can certainly free the Christian musicians from Wall Street. As for your broader question, great art and music rises in ages when their patrons are motivated by something other than making money. A king, a bishop, a rich merchant, a city, a monastery, shells out the money for art and music and architecture for their own glory or the glory of their kingdom or their community or their god, not because they're trying to rake in bucks -- and glory is what they get. Those who just want to make money make money; in the words of a certain rabbi, "they have their reward."

replies.title: Re: Bibliomancy
replies.content: If I may make a few suggestions: You can do bibliomancy with books other than the Bible if you’re not inclined to it particularly. comments.title: Pisces, Cancer, and Scorpio
comments.content:
I forget which post it was -- maybe last Magic Monday -- but not that long ago you were discussing the Age of Pisces and mentioned that one of the characteristics was a tendency to pull in one of two directions only to snap back, and you related this to the symbol of Pisces, which has two sides facing away from each other, as if pulling away, but tethered together by the horizontal bar.

I was contemplating what the symbol of Cancer -- with the two figures circling round each other, almost yin-yang-like -- might suggest, when it hit me: Pisces is sloshing water, trying to go this way or that but always sloshing back the other way.

Cancer is a whirlpool, drawing everything toward the center. Suggesting that the Age of Cancer should have been marked by a centralization of human societies into early civilizations, and a settling down to home and hearth instead of nomadic life.

Following those gives me a hint on Scorpio (though I haven't yet connected this thought to the symbol): Scorpio is still water with violent currents underneath, which fits with both the traditional rulership of Mars (soldiers maneuvering under the cover of darkness) and the brief rulership by Pluto (the underworld). Which suggests that the Age of Scorpio, when it comes, could be one of cold war after cold war, and a new golden age for secret societies.

comments.title: Visitors during ritual
comments.content:
Hi JMG!

I've been doing the LBRP and MPR daily for over a year now, and very often, especially when I do it after dark, or when my concentration is not at its best, visions of rather strange little creatures appear before my mind's eye (I do the practices with eyes closed). These critters look chimeric, oftentimes rather funny or silly, and resemble cartoons more than actual animals. They are usually smaller than me and appear one at a time, outside the perimeter of the pentagrams, and on their own accord.
This happens mostly after the LBRP part is done, during the MPR, and never outside of the ritual context.

Usually I concentrate on my visualisation, and all of a sudden, there's some trunk-nosed 3-eyed purple cat looking at me, Or some bat-monkey with its eyes on stalks. They look really rather endearing in their bizarre ways, and never threatening.

They don't come close and they don't interfere, and when I tell them to leave (with my mind's voice), they disappear, often leaving a sensation of something having left and me being alone again. Their presence is much like an intelligent animal's, like a bird or a monkey, in terms of the degree of interest and understanding I feel coming from them. So far, I haven't noticed one trying to communicate with me in any way.

Since the inhabitants of the other planes are often mentioned in your work, but situations like these aren't dealt with in LRM, I'm wondering

1) What's going on here, is astral fauna visiting me, attracted by the light of my MPR? Or is some part of me sabotaging my practices? They sure seem objective and self-animated.

2) Assuming others have similar axperiences, do these beings appear differently to people? I could imagine the beholder's mind with its own visual library is involved in giving them the equivalent of a visual appearance.

3) I'd like to get to know them better, but the middle of the ritual is not the best time for that. How would I go about that, and is that even advisable?

Thanks for your answers, and for all your work. I've got a copy of 10 Billion in the mail and am giddy with excitement like 5 year old before christmas :D

P.S. I'm posting from Europe, and, given the difference in our time zones and biorhythms, I oftentimes don't get to read your reply before MM is over. So thanks in advance!

replies.title: Re: Visitors during ritual

5. Repeat the process, tracing a line around to the north, where you draw the Water form of the invoking planetary hexagram of Venus and vibrating the Name. Finish by vibrating ARARITA.

6. Trace a line around to the East, completing the circle, and return to the center. Stand, facing east, and perform the Analysis of INRI, saying the following out loud:

I, N, R, I.
Yod, Nun, Resh, Yod.
Virgo, Isis, might mother. Scorpio, Apophis, the destroyer. Sol, Osiris, slain and risen.
Isis, Apophis, Osiris.
I, A, O
IAO (vibrated)

Raise your arms to the sides, making the Sign of Osiris Slain. I tried out a lot of different money-making ideas, without much success.

From what I've seen, in the online entrepreneur community this is very common, and has to be overcome if you want to get anywhere.

One thing I've started to internalize recently is JOOO (Joy of Opting out) > FOMO (fear of missing out). I got this from some "internet entrepreneurs", I won't quote their names here in case it triggers FOMO, but I find that the better ones in this space actually say "I'd be glad if after watching this video, you decide to delete all your social media accounts and get to work".

Many things out there, whether it's deliberate marketing, or just people talking about what they find cool and interesting, will trigger FOMO in you. You decide "Oh, this looks a lot shinier than this thing I'm working on, let's jump over", then the cycle repeats. Instead, you tell yourself you choose to opt out, the grass isn't greener on the other side, it's greener where you water it.

comments.content:
I've taken to tracking lunar transits to points in my chart to try to get a sense of how they work in my life. I'm currently tracking transits to Pluto, and since I have extensive notes on them, realized I need to amend my comment that Pluto has weakened in my life: it hasn't, rather it's manifesting as "Minerva". Minerva then appears to be very different from Pluto: it's sensual, lovely, focused on beauty and pleasures, but all highly physical ones. For example, noticing the roar of the river I live near and the sound of frogs croaking; or one of the best meals I've ever had. It appears to me to be the Earth-Pull.

I've read the chapter in the Cosmic Doctrine where Fortune discusses the attraction to outer space and have to agree it fits Pluto to a T, but not Minerva. But, it's implied but never quite stated that one view of the attraction to outer space is the desire to descend deeper into matter than the Nadir. Which would seem to suggest that if Pluto were the Earth-Pull, the desire to descend into matter, then following it would lead to outer space; while at the higher levels it could be quite enjoyable: thus Pluto and Minerva.

This also explains why people with strong Plutos tend to have Pluto, and not Minerva: Minerva followed to her logical end transforms into Pluto. In other words, people with strong Plutos will feel the Earth-Pull so much that they end up on the edge, staring into outer space, while those with weaker Plutos will have an easier time not getting sucked in that far. I think it also explains why Plutonian phenomena, such as cinema and TV, seem to serve to shut down the higher capacities in human beings.

1) Does this line of thinking make sense to you? Is there something you think I'm missing?

2) I think this will be a good theme for meditation; I assume I'll need to focus on the beautiful and the spiritual in order to balance out the resulting atraction of my thoughts towards the Plutonian Earth-Pull by these meditations; are there other things you think I should do to be safe?

3) Speaking of meditation, I've recently realized I've been meditating when I walk since I was fairly young. I'm assuming this is a legacy of my past occultist life, but it raises a question: since I'm still working through LRM, I've been taking the themes given once a day, during which time I sit to meditate; and allowing myself to explore other material when I walk (since walking is my preferred form of travel, and I enjoy being outside in nice weather even if I have nowhere to go, I do it a decent amount); I assume there isn't a problem with this, but I would like to make sure.

3a) Is there an upper limit to how much meditation can be done safely?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's an intriguing meditation, certainly.

2) Another excellent theme!

3) It should be fine.

3a) That depends entirely on individual factors. Discursive mediation is safer than most other kinds of meditation, too.
